author: garyrong
content: True. But we already did it even before the Amsterdam, We refund `params.CallNewAccountGas - params.TxAuthTupleGas)` before also.

author: misilva73
content:
> I think one thing I disagree is: we can't use the one rule in mempool validation while another rule in block processing. If we need to enforce this property with a consistent precondition, then the validation must be stateless.
I agree with this and this was my main concern

author: misilva73
content: On this, we were trying to not change how execution refunds work today. We did change how state creation refunds work, and that is already a big change. I would rather think about execution gas refunds in a more holistic way in a dedicated eip

author: misilva73
content: I think we are agreeing that we want to have a resource-based refund for auths and the discussino has mostly been on how to do it. I still find it strange to change the intrinsic costs, even though it seems easier to explain. I would rather do it to gas_left and reservoir using the same LIFO approach

author: misilva73
content: would that be ok for you, <@1252939266871263294> ? Should I update the EIP in a new PR or do you prefer to update your PR instead?

author: lu_pinto
content:
I'm still not fully convinced about the `ACCOUNT_WRITE` case though.

> True. But we already did it even before the Amsterdam, We refund params.CallNewAccountGas - params.TxAuthTupleGas) before also.

This supposedly includes state also, right? We already changed the state part which is the biggest chunk of the cost. So to be clear, before Amsterdam state + regular went to the refund at the end. After Amsterdam, only regular goes to the refund counter. I do not fully follow the point you're trying to make.

We are aligned on the source based refund but where my stance differs is to label these 7702 overcharges a "refund". The user is not being refunded, it is being overcharged for validation purposes. That is fine as it needs to post more gas for the transaction to succeed but then what I don't think is right is to charge for work that it's not doing. The same way Gary suggested to add back overcharges related to invalid authorisations or already processed authorisations. This is also new and rightly fixed with 8037 and I stand behind that decision on my side. It just feels like there is a dual criteria on how to charge for these 7702 delegations.
